what_it_does: Records a design-guidance noticeboard item (section 6 of a larger document) about modeling a document tree as an addressable typed tree: it warns against hand-rolling an AST, explains the heading-scoping trap, requires stable per-node identifiers with a text-quote fallback, and proposes addressable insertion zones between sibling nodes.
observations: YAML frontmatter declares id, address, type, source, title, author session, channel, links, and history.
The body is a numbered design section titled around document trees and AST handling.
It states that flat blocks cannot answer 'everything under this heading' and that a real typed tree is required.
Heading containment must be synthesized from a real parser such as CommonMark/remark/mdast or a DOM walk, not recomputed heuristically per tap.
It recommends minting a stable id per node and keying envelopes to it, with a text-quote anchor as a recovery fallback.
It proposes modeling insertion zones as addressable edges between two sibling node ids rather than as nodes or positional gaps.
It cites Notion, Hypothesis, Google Docs insertion-point comments, and code-review whole-file comments as precedents.
standouts: The explicit warning that heading-scoping is a sharp edge because headings are flat siblings, not containers.
The requirement for stable node ids plus quote fallback instead of positional paths.
The proposal of addressable zones as sibling-edge anchors.
conventions: Uses YAML frontmatter with id, address, links, and history for ledger metadata.
Uses Markdown section headings and bold labels for named guidance.
References external products as design precedents.
No code samples or schema definitions; guidance is prose-only.
concerns: The title in frontmatter appears truncated and contains an unbalanced quote: '...watch the "what''s un'.
Terms like BD-C and 'envelope' are used without being defined in this file.
The guidance is abstract and lacks a concrete data model or example AST shape.
notes: A design note arguing that reliable document addressing needs a parsed AST, stable ids, and explicit zones rather than positional paths or heading heuristics.
questions: What is BD-C in this context?
What exactly is the 'envelope' being keyed to node ids?
How are stable ids minted and persisted across re-parses?
How are zones rendered or interacted with in the UI?
What is the parent document or thread this item is part of?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to give document-tree design guidance, and the content does exactly that.
apparent_themes: Explicit containment derived from a real AST
intention_signals: It is trying to set non-negotiable design constraints for a document/annotation system so that comments and envelopes survive edits and re-parsing.
novelty: The explicit proposal to treat insertion zones as addressable edges anchored between two sibling node ids, rather than as nodes or positional gaps.
contribution: It contributes concrete design guidance for building a reparse-resilient document model: synthesize heading containment from a real parser AST, mint stable ids per node with a text-quote fallback, and model insertion zones as sibling-edge anchors.
summary_for_embedding: Design-guidance noticeboard item prescribing an addressable typed document tree built from a real AST, using stable node ids with quote fallback and sibling-edge zones.
intention_for_embedding: To prevent brittle positional addressing by mandating parser-derived containment, stable identities, and addressable gaps between blocks.
